title: Context Isolation Between Sub-Tasks
concept: Giving each sub-agent or sub-task a fresh, scoped context instead of one ever-growing shared window.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: what was a 'scratchpad' in the last lesson?

options.text: A separate working-memory space where the agent jots down intermediate reasoning, apart from the main context
A tool that deletes the agent's memory after every step
A shared document that every sub-agent edits at the same time

exercises.explain: A scratchpad is the agent's own private working-memory buffer for jotting down intermediate thoughts, kept separate from the main context window so that space stays clean.

cards.title: One Giant Messy Folder?
cards.body: Imagine an agent juggling five sub-tasks, all dumping their notes into the SAME context window. Soon the agent can't tell which notes belong to which task — everything blurs into one overloaded mess.

cards.title: What Is Context Isolation?
cards.body: Context isolation means giving each sub-agent or sub-task its own fresh, scoped context window instead of one giant shared window that keeps growing. Each sub-task only sees what it needs to do its job — nothing more.

cards.title: Separate Kitchens, One Restaurant
cards.body: Imagine one chef trying to cook appetizers, mains, and desserts all on the same tiny cutting board without ever clearing it — crumbs and sauces from every dish pile up together. Instead, a good restaurant gives each station its own clean counter. Sub-agents work the same way: each gets its own clean 'counter' (context) for its one job.

cards.title: A Report-Writing Agent
cards.body: A 'write market report' agent spins up three sub-agents: one researches competitors, one analyzes pricing data, one drafts the summary. Each sub-agent gets ONLY its own relevant instructions and data — the pricing sub-agent never sees the competitor research chatter. Each returns a clean, short result to the main agent, which combines them.

cards.title: Myth: More Shared Context = Better Teamwork
cards.body: It seems like sharing everything would help sub-agents 'stay on the same page.' Actually, dumping all context into one shared window causes confusion, wasted tokens, and sub-agents accidentally reacting to irrelevant info from other tasks. Scoped, isolated context keeps each sub-task sharp and focused.

cards.title: Shared Window vs Isolated Windows
cards.body: Compare a single overloaded shared context to several small, clean, task-specific contexts.

left.text: All sub-agents dump notes into one window — it swells with irrelevant info and gets confusing fast.

right.text: Each sub-agent gets its own small, clean window with just what it needs, then reports back a tidy result.

exercises.prompt: Why is context isolation useful when running multiple sub-agents?

options.text: It makes the system use more tokens overall, which improves accuracy
It keeps each sub-agent focused on its own task without noise from unrelated sub-tasks
It forces all sub-agents to memorize the entire conversation history
It guarantees sub-agents never make mistakes

exercises.explain: Context isolation keeps each sub-agent's window clean and relevant to its specific job, so it isn't distracted or confused by information meant for a different sub-task.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Isolating context means each sub-agent should get access to the full history of every other sub-agent's work, just in case.
exercises.explain: False — context isolation means the opposite: each sub-agent only gets what's relevant to its own scoped task, not the full history of everything else.

exercises.sentence: Each sub-agent should get a fresh, scoped context instead of sharing one ever-growing window.
exercises.explain: The word 'scoped' means each sub-agent's context is limited to exactly what it needs for its task — nothing extra, nothing from unrelated sub-tasks.

exercises.prompt: Which of these three system designs was most likely written by someone who does NOT understand context isolation?

options.text: Design A: Every sub-agent shares one single context window that keeps growing with all sub-agents' messages combined
Design B: Each sub-agent spins up with a fresh context containing only its assigned task and needed data
Design C: Sub-agents return short summaries to a parent agent, which combines the clean results

exercises.explain: Design A is the flawed one — a single ever-growing shared window is exactly the anti-pattern context isolation fixes, since it lets noise from unrelated sub-tasks pile up and confuse every sub-agent.

exercises.prompt: An agent system has a Research sub-agent, a Coding sub-agent, and a Testing sub-agent working on a multi-step project. What's the BEST context strategy?

options.text: One shared context window that all three sub-agents read and write to continuously
Each sub-agent gets a fresh, scoped context with only what it needs, then returns a clean result to the parent
Give every sub-agent the entire chat history from the very first user message, unfiltered
Let each sub-agent invent its own instructions since context doesn't matter for smaller tasks

exercises.explain: Option B is correct: isolating context per sub-task keeps each sub-agent focused, prevents irrelevant details from one job leaking into another, and avoids the shared window bloating with unrelated noise. The parent agent then only needs each sub-agent's clean summary, not their messy internal process — this mirrors real engineering teams passing along finished reports, not their entire meeting notes.
